reasoning_summary: summary: The panel achieved perfect consensus with all four panelists estimating the global AI consulting market will reach $18.5 billion USD in 24 months. This represents approximately 35% compound annual growth from a current baseline of $9-11 billion, signaling a major inflection point as enterprises transition from exploratory AI pilots to scaled operational integration. The unanimity across C-suite perspectives (CEO, CFO, CTO, CMO) suggests strong conviction that this market expansion is driven by genuine business necessity rather than speculative hype, though panelists acknowledge meaningful downside and upside scenarios.
key_agreements: [0] Current baseline market size is approximately $9-11 billion USD, with robust 30-35% CAGR growth over 24 months
[1] Enterprises are transitioning from pilot/exploratory phases to production-scale implementation, creating sustained consulting demand
[2] Major consulting firms (Accenture, Deloitte, MBB) will capture significant market share due to enterprise preference for established partners
[3] Executive FOMO and competitive pressure are primary drivers of consulting spend, independent of clear ROI proof
[4] Both upside and downside scenarios are plausible, with symmetric $14-25B confidence intervals reflecting genuine uncertainty despite point estimate consensus
key_disagreements: [0] Drivers of future consulting demand: CTO emphasizes technical debt and security/compliance challenges (build-to-buy shift), while CMO emphasizes brand differentiation and FOMO-driven decision-making
[1] Risk factors: CEO/CFO focus on macroeconomic headwinds and CapEx delays, while CTO specifically calls out regulatory catalysts (EU AI Act) as potential explosive growth multipliers
[2] Internal capability assessment: CFO implies organizations lack internal AI expertise, while CMO suggests potential for 'rapid internal upskilling' as a constraining factor on consulting demand
evolution_notes: This was a single-round estimation exercise, so no evolution across rounds is observable. The perfect convergence (IQR = 0.0, all estimates = $18.5B) on the first round is notable and suggests either: (1) strong alignment in C-suite perspectives on market fundamentals, (2) potential anchoring effects if a preliminary estimate was shared, or (3) inherent difficulty in distinguishing between $17-20B scenarios when the underlying growth thesis is broadly accepted. The wide symmetric confidence intervals ($14-25B) reflect genuine uncertainty about execution speed and exogenous shocks, despite consensus on the central tendency.
